title: Medicare provider numbers and the Section 19AB 10-year moratorium
metaDescription: How Medicare provider numbers and Section 19AB's 10-year moratorium affect where IMGs can bill Medicare, plus DPA, DWS, exemptions and scaling.
summaryMarkdown: To bill the Medicare Benefits Schedule (MBS), a doctor needs a Medicare provider number, and a separate number applies for each location they practise at. [[cite:provider-number-billing]] For many IMGs, access to Medicare benefits is also restricted under Section 19AB of the *Health Insurance Act 1973*, the '10-year moratorium'. [[cite:moratorium-19ab]] During the moratorium, an IMG generally needs to work where they're most needed, a Distribution Priority Area (DPA) for GPs, or a District of Workforce Shortage (DWS) for non-GP specialists, to access Medicare benefits there. [[cite:dpa-dws-where]] This page is general information, separate from registration and visa requirements; always confirm your own situation against the official Services Australia and Department of Health pages.

flow.label: You need a provider number
flow.note: Required to bill Medicare for your services.

flow.label: Section 19AB restricts most IMGs
flow.note: A 10-year moratorium from your first registration in Australia.

flow.label: Work in a DPA or DWS location
flow.note: DPA for GPs, DWS for non-GP specialists, to gain access.

flow.label: Remote areas count faster
flow.note: Scaling under the Modified Monash Model reduces the time.

flow.label: Unrestricted Medicare access
flow.note: After the moratorium, or once an exemption is granted.

sections.heading: What a provider number is and why it matters
sections.bodyMarkdown:
A Medicare provider number lets a doctor claim payments under the MBS; without one you can't bill MBS items or have benefits paid for the services you provide. [[cite:provider-number-billing]]

Provider numbers are tied to **where** you practise: you must use the number for the location where you provide the services, and you need a separate number for each location. [[cite:provider-number-location]] That location link is what makes Section 19AB matter in practice, for a restricted IMG, whether benefits are payable can depend on the classification of the specific location.

sections.heading: Section 19AB and the 10-year moratorium
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Section 19AB prevents the payment of Medicare benefits to overseas trained doctors (OTDs) and foreign graduates of an accredited medical school (FGAMS) for a minimum of ten years from the date of their first registration as a medical practitioner in Australia. [[cite:moratorium-19ab]]

Services Australia frames who is affected by a 1997 cut-off: the restriction applies to IMGs who obtained their first registration, or became a permanent resident or citizen, on or after 1 January 1997, and then lasts 10 years. [[cite:moratorium-1997-trigger]] Those who, before 1 January 1997, had registered with a state or territory medical board and kept a continuing right to remain are excluded. [[cite:moratorium-pre1997-excluded]] For permanent residents and citizens, the restrictions end after 10 years; for someone who remains a temporary resident, they continue. [[cite:moratorium-end-residency]] This is separate from registration (e.g. Section 19AA) and from visa conditions.

sections.heading: DPA for GPs vs DWS for non-GP specialists
sections.bodyMarkdown:
During the moratorium you generally access Medicare benefits by working where you're most needed; the classification depends on whether you're a GP or another specialist. [[cite:dpa-dws-where]]

- **Distribution Priority Area (DPA)** applies to GPs, locations with a shortage of GP services, comparing a catchment against a benchmark. [[cite:dpa-definition]]
- **District of Workforce Shortage (DWS)** applies to non-GP specialists, areas with poor access to specialists, measured using population and Medicare billing data. [[cite:dws-definition]]

Check a specific address with the Department of Health's [Health Workforce Locator](/where-you-can-work). [[cite:workforce-locator]] Classifications are reviewed periodically (for example, DPA updated March 2025, DWS December 2023), so always check the current status. [[cite:classifications-updated]]

sections.heading: Section 19AB exemptions
sections.bodyMarkdown: An exemption lets a restricted IMG access Medicare benefits before the moratorium ends. The most reliable way to get an indefinite exemption is to work in a DPA (GPs) or DWS (non-GP specialists) location. [[cite:exemption-dpa-dws]] Exemptions are assessed in connection with provider numbers: Services Australia assesses you when you apply for an initial provider number, and again each time you apply for a new one. [[cite:exemption-assessed-provider-number]] It may take up to 28 days for the department to assess an application. [[cite:exemption-28-days]]

sections.heading: Scaling: reducing time in a DPA or DWS
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Scaling is a non-cash incentive for overseas trained doctors who want to reduce their moratorium period. [[cite:scaling-incentive]] Working in eligible locations earns 'scaling credits', the more remote the area, the more credits, and the more credits you accumulate, the sooner you can work in locations not classified as DPA or DWS. [[cite:scaling-credits-remote]] Enough credits can earn a class exemption for the rest of your moratorium. [[cite:scaling-class-exemption]]

Remoteness for scaling uses the Modified Monash Model (MM 1 major city to MM 7 very remote). [[cite:mmm-scale]] Importantly, scaling can shorten time in a DPA/DWS, but the moratorium is always a minimum of 10 years. [[cite:scaling-10-year-minimum]] You can track scaling and your location exemptions through Health Professional Online Services (HPOS). [[cite:scaling-hpos]]

claims.text: A Medicare provider number is required to claim under the MBS; a doctor must have one to bill MBS items.
A doctor must use the provider number for the location where they provide the services and needs a separate provider number for each location.
Section 19AB prevents payment of Medicare benefits to overseas trained doctors and FGAMS for a minimum of ten years from first registration in Australia (the 10-year moratorium).
Services Australia states the restriction applies to IMGs who got first registration, or became a permanent resident or citizen, on or after 1 January 1997, lasting 10 years.
Practitioners who registered with a state or territory board before 1 January 1997 and kept a continuing right to remain are excluded from the Section 19AB restrictions.
The Section 19AB restrictions end after 10 years for a permanent resident or citizen, but continue for someone who remains a temporary resident.
To access Medicare benefits during the moratorium, an IMG generally works in a DPA (GPs) or a DWS (non-GP specialists).
DPA identifies locations with a shortage of GP services, comparing a GP catchment against a benchmark.
DWS identifies areas with poor access to non-GP specialists, measured using population and Medicare billing data.
The Department of Health's Health Workforce Locator lets users check the classification of a specific location.
The DPA was updated in March 2025 and the DWS in December 2023.
An exemption lets an IMG access Medicare benefits before the moratorium ends; the best way to get an indefinite exemption is to work in a DPA or DWS location.
Services Australia assesses an IMG for a 19AB exemption when they apply for an initial provider number and each time they apply for a new one.
It may take up to 28 days for the department to assess an exemption application.
Scaling is a non-cash incentive for overseas trained doctors who want to reduce their moratorium period.
Working in eligible locations earns scaling credits (more remote areas earn more), and more credits let a doctor work outside DPA/DWS sooner.
Enough scaling credits can earn a class exemption for the rest of the moratorium.
The Modified Monash Model classifies remoteness from MM 1 (major city) to MM 7 (very remote).
Scaling can reduce time worked in a DPA or DWS, but the moratorium is always a minimum of 10 years.
Overseas trained doctors can track scaling and view their 19AB location exemptions through HPOS.

faq.question: What is the Section 19AB '10-year moratorium'?
faq.answerMarkdown: A restriction under Section 19AB of the *Health Insurance Act 1973* that prevents payment of Medicare benefits to overseas trained doctors and FGAMS for at least ten years from first registration. To access benefits during this time, doctors generally work in a DPA (GPs) or DWS (non-GP specialists) location.

faq.question: Does it apply to every IMG?
faq.answerMarkdown: No. Services Australia describes it as applying to IMGs who got first registration, or became a permanent resident or citizen, on or after 1 January 1997. Those registered before 1 January 1997 with a continuing right to remain are excluded. Check your own situation with Services Australia.

faq.question: What's the difference between DPA and DWS?
faq.answerMarkdown: DPA applies to GPs (locations short of GP services); DWS applies to non-GP specialists (areas with poor specialist access). Check a specific location with the Health Workforce Locator.

faq.question: How do I get a 19AB exemption?
faq.answerMarkdown: An exemption lets you access Medicare benefits before the moratorium ends; the most reliable indefinite exemption comes from working in a DPA or DWS location. Services Australia assesses you when you apply for a provider number.

faq.question: What is 'scaling' and can it shorten the moratorium?
faq.answerMarkdown: Scaling is a non-cash incentive for doctors who want to reduce their moratorium. Working in eligible (more remote) locations earns credits that let you work outside DPA/DWS sooner, potentially with a class exemption, but the moratorium is always a minimum of 10 years.

faq.question: Is this the same as my registration or visa?
faq.answerMarkdown: No. The 19AB moratorium governs access to Medicare benefits and is separate from medical registration (including Section 19AA) and from your visa conditions. Each is administered separately.
